    "R1.3": "Claims are directly contradicted by the artifact's own numbers. The sentence 'The 9.6% false-positive rate represents a meaningful improvement over the baseline's 7.2%' inverts the direction of the result. Evidence-claim alignment is load-bearing here.",

  "clarification_question": "Should 'make this stronger' include correcting logical and evidential errors — specifically, the text claims BioCrew outperforms the baseline but the reported numbers show it performs worse on both FPR and FNR — or is scope limited to prose quality and argumentative framing while treating the data and claims as fixed inputs?"

rationale.R1.1: The contribution claim — that multi-agent role specialization is more reliable — is built on results that invert its conclusion; any genuine strengthening must reconcile or honestly restate the central thesis.
rationale.R1.2: The evaluation section is internally incoherent: BioCrew's FPR (9.6%) and FNR (13.4%) both exceed the baseline's (7.2% / 11.8%), yet the text asserts 'substantially reduces both error modes.' This is a load-bearing logical failure; the argument collapses without resolving it.
rationale.R1.3: Claims are directly contradicted by the artifact's own numbers. The sentence 'The 9.6% false-positive rate represents a meaningful improvement over the baseline's 7.2%' inverts the direction of the result. Evidence-claim alignment is load-bearing here.
rationale.R1.6: Role specialization is defined in near-identical language four times across three paragraphs; prose compression and clarity are natural and expected components of 'making it stronger.'

parsed.clarification_question: Should 'make this stronger' include correcting logical and evidential errors — specifically, the text claims BioCrew outperforms the baseline but the reported numbers show it performs worse on both FPR and FNR — or is scope limited to prose quality and argumentative framing while treating the data and claims as fixed inputs?
